questions.question: Explain the difference between 'su' and 'sudo' commands in Linux.
keyPoints: su requires root password, sudo uses user's own password
sudo provides better security through configuration and audit trail
sudo can limit specific commands per user
su switches to a new user session, sudo executes single command

questions.question: What is the purpose of the /etc/sudoers file and why should you use visudo to edit it?
keyPoints: /etc/sudoers defines which users can use sudo
It specifies what commands users can execute with elevated privileges
visudo performs syntax checking before saving
Prevents lockout from malformed configurations

questions.question: Explain the difference between 'su', 'su -', and 'sudo -i' commands.
keyPoints: su - switches user with login shell (loads full environment)
su - switches user without loading new environment
sudo -i starts interactive root shell using user's password
All three achieve similar result of becoming root but differ in password and environment

questions.question: Why is it recommended to use sudo instead of logging in as root directly?
keyPoints: Better security - doesn't share root password
Audit trail - logs which user ran commands
Time-limited privileges - sudo session expires
Can limit command access per user
Prevents accidental destructive commands

questions.question: What is the wheel group in Linux and what is its significance?
keyPoints: Wheel group is a special administrative group
Members can use 'su' command to become root
Provides controlled access to superuser privileges
Adding users to wheel is more secure than giving direct root access

questions.question: Describe the root user's home directory and explain its significance.
keyPoints: Root's home directory is /root (not /home/root)
Separate from regular users' /home/<username>
Contains root's personal files, configurations, and scripts
Signifies root's special status in the filesystem hierarchy

questions.question: Explain what the prompt symbols $ and # indicate in Linux.
keyPoints: $ indicates regular user shell
# indicates root (superuser) shell
$ represents user-level permissions and restrictions
# represents full administrative privileges
Helps users identify their current privilege level

questions.question: Explain in detail the various methods to become the superuser (root) in Linux. Compare their advantages and disadvantages.
keyPoints: Method 1: su command - requires root password, switches user, loads environment based on flags
Method 2: sudo su - uses user's password if permitted, achieves same result as su
Method 3: sudo -i - interactive login shell as root using user's password
Method 4: sudo -s - starts shell with elevated privileges
Method 5: sudo <command> - single command execution
Advantages of sudo over su: uses own password, audit trail, time-limited, configurable
Security implications of each method
Best practices for different scenarios

questions.question: Discuss the security aspects and best practices related to root user access in Linux systems.
keyPoints: Never share root password - use sudo instead
Direct root login provides no audit trail
Use visudo to prevent configuration errors
Implement wheel group for controlled su access
Sudo timeout balances security and convenience
Regular audit of /var/log/auth.log or sudo logs
Use key-based authentication for remote root access
Consider sudoers configuration for command restrictions
Root login via SSH should be disabled (PermitRootLogin no)
Create separate admin accounts for daily use

questions.question: Explain the Linux user privilege model with special reference to UID 0 and how the superuser differs from regular users.
keyPoints: Every user has unique UID (User Identifier)
UID 0 is hardcoded as root user in Linux kernel
UID 0 has bypass of all permission checks
Regular users have UIDs typically > 1000
UID determines privilege level in kernel-level operations
Root can access any file, execute any command, modify any resource
GID 0 (root group) similarly has special status
Supplementary groups further control access
User database stored in /etc/passwd and /etc/shadow
Process privileges tied to UID/GID of executing user

questions.question: A new system administrator needs to grant sudo access to a user. Explain the step-by-step procedure including configuration file syntax.
keyPoints: Add user to sudo group (usermod -aG sudo username) or wheel group
Or edit /etc/sudoers using visudo for custom configuration
Sudoers syntax: username ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL
First ALL: run from any host
(ALL:ALL): run as any user, any group
ALL: run any command
Alternative: %groupname ALL=(ALL:ALL) ALL for groups
Test configuration with sudo -l command
Explain timestamp timeout behavior
Document the access granted
